p: This document describes day-to-day operations: monitoring, alarms, troubleshooting, scheduled maintenance, and disaster recovery considerations. It assumes the platform is deployed as described in 12 Deployment.
Two Amazon CloudWatch dashboards are recommended, with metrics drawn from CloudWatch metrics for ALB, API Gateway, CloudFront, ECS, Lambda, Step Functions, DynamoDB, and S3.
For platform operators. Tracks the health of the components themselves.

tr.td: ALB request rate, 4xx/5xx counts, p50/p95/p99 latency
Detect traffic anomalies and latency drift
API Gateway HTTP API request rate, integration latency, authoriser duration
CloudFront cache hit rate, edge request counts, byte counts
Fargate service task counts, CPU/memory utilisation, scaling actions
Lambda authoriser invocations, errors, cold-start init duration
Step Functions executions started/succeeded/failed, current running

p: For platform owners and users.
A small set of alarms covers the failure modes that need human attention.

tr.td: Backend errors, capacity, or configuration issue
Slow responses; possible cold start storm or saturation

p: Each alarm should link to a runbook section below.

tr.td: API Gateway HTTP API access logs
userId, path, status, response length, latency
Edge location, bytes, URI, status, user agent
Per-dataset events (jobs, schema changes, SQL edits)

p: Useful queries to keep handy, expressed in CloudWatch Logs Insights syntax against the API Gateway access-log group:
Top users by request count (last 24 hours):

ac:structured-macro.ac:plain-text-body: filter userId != '' and userId != 'anonymous' | stats count(*) as requests by userId | sort requests desc | limit 20

p: Data egress per user:

ac:structured-macro.ac:plain-text-body: filter userId != '' and userId != 'anonymous' | stats sum(responseLength) as bytes by userId | sort bytes desc

p: Feature downloads by dataset:

ac:structured-macro.ac:plain-text-body: filter path like '/features/v1/collections/' | stats count(*) as requests by path | sort requests desc

p: Job failures by dataset:

ac:structured-macro.ac:plain-text-body: filter status = 'failed' | stats count(*) as failures by dataset_id | sort failures desc

p: Check container service logs and event history. The most common causes:

ol.li: Image not present — verify the image tag is in the image registry.
Task role permissions — verify the task IAM role can reach the key-value store and object storage.
Resource starvation — check whether the container cluster has capacity.
Health check failing — confirm the service is binding to the expected port and responding to the health endpoint.

p: The job has been created but the workflow has not started:

ol.li: Check the dataset's pipeline_status — if another job is running, the new job is queued, not pending.
Check the workflow engine's execution history for the dataset — if no execution exists, the editing API's call to start the workflow may have failed silently.
Inspect the editing API logs for the corresponding job ID.
If genuinely stuck, manually invoke the workflow with the job's payload; mark the original job failed if duplicate is unsafe.

h2: Pipeline job stuck in validating or generating
p: The container task has been launched but has not completed:

ol.li: List running container tasks for the validation or generation task definition.
Tail the task's logs to see where it is.
If the task has been running longer than the timeout, mark it failed via the failure handler.
If the task is making progress (logs continue), let it complete; check whether the workflow engine's per-task timeout is appropriately set.

p: Messages in the validation DLQ indicate uploads that failed to be parsed and never started a workflow execution:

ol.li: Inspect the message bodies — they contain the original storage event.
Identify the offending object in landing/.
Determine the cause (corrupt file, unexpected format, missing job record).
If the upload was legitimate, manually trigger the workflow with the correct payload.
Delete the DLQ messages once handled.

p: Check, in order:

ol.li: Was the token issued by a trusted issuer? The trusted-issuers list is a deployment parameter.
Has the token expired?
Has the user's policy been recently changed? Cache TTL is up to 5 minutes; wait or invalidate.
Is the user's IdP group recognised by the ceiling table? An unknown group results in a denied request.
For API keys: is the key still active in the keys table?

h2: CDN returning stale tiles after a promotion
p: Either:

ul.li: The CDN invalidation in the promotion function did not complete; check the function logs.
The tile server cache has not picked up the new ETag; restart the affected task or wait for the cache TTL.

h2: Cognito (or other IdP) user cannot sign in
p: For Cognito specifically:

ol.li: Verify the user exists in the user pool.
Verify the user is CONFIRMED (not still FORCE_CHANGE_PASSWORD if invited).
Check the app client's allowed auth flows.

p: For other IdPs, the relevant checks are IdP-specific and outside the platform's control.
Either:

ul.li: A pathological access pattern (a hot partition); inspect the most-accessed partitions and consider splitting the workload.
A sudden traffic spike beyond on-demand capacity; check the cloud's provisioned/on-demand setting.
Cascading retries from a transient cloud-side issue; back off and retry.

tr.td: Compact per-job history into monthly archives
Compact per-job events into monthly archives

p: The single S3 bucket carries an S3 Lifecycle policy that transitions objects to S3 Intelligent-Tiering after 30 days. Recommended additional rules (also documented in 04 Data Layout):

ul.li: landing/ — delete after job completion (handled by promotion Lambda; lifecycle rule as backstop after 7 days).
pmtiles/staging/ — delete after promotion (handled by promotion Lambda; lifecycle rule after 1 day).

drafts/ — delete after 90 days regardless of session status. This governs the content files (delta and diff PMTiles, validation results) in S3. It is distinct from the DynamoDB TTL on the edit-sessions table, which expires the session record after one month of inactivity (non-terminal) or six months (terminal). The two mechanisms can run on different schedules without coordination — a session whose drafts content has been lifecycle-deleted can still be inspected via its DynamoDB record and re-generated if needed.
Old history/ archives — transition to S3 Glacier Deep Archive after one year for datasets with long retention requirements.

tr.td: Hours — re-bootstrap from IaC; data is intact in object storage and the key-value store
Loss of an entire cloud region
Hours plus data restore — requires cross-region replication of object storage and key-value store

p: DR drills should exercise:

ol.li: Re-deploying the IaC into a clean account against an existing data store. The expectation is that the platform is fully operational after the deployment completes.
Restoring the key-value store to a point in time and verifying authorisation policies and dataset registries are intact.
For high-availability deployments: failing over to a secondary region (if cross-region replication is configured).

p: The platform's design — stateless services, data in durable storage — makes the deployment-side of DR a normal redeploy. The data side relies on the durability and recoverability of the chosen object storage and key-value store, both of which offer multi-AZ durability by default.
Two dimensions to plan:

Read traffic is dominated by tile requests. CDN cache hit rate is the leading indicator: at high hit rates, backend load is decoupled from request volume. Capacity for the vector and raster tile servers should be planned for the unique tile rate (cache-miss rate × request rate), not the total request rate.

Edit traffic is dominated by workflow executions. Validation and generation tasks scale per execution; the limiting factor is concurrent workflow executions and container cluster capacity. Plan for peak concurrent edit submissions across all datasets.
The authoriser and the function-runtime services scale per request; their cost is proportional to request count, with negligible per-instance overhead.

In off mode, baseline cost approaches the storage and registry minimums. Storage cost is proportional to data volume; key-value store on-demand costs are proportional to request count; the CDN charges for transfer.

In minimal mode, baseline cost adds the always-running container service tasks (one per service group's services). For a deployment with vector tiles, query layer, and editing enabled, this is a small number of tasks at modest sizing.
In performance mode, baseline cost adds the pre-warmed task count at the larger CPU/memory allocations. This is the highest steady-state cost.

A meaningful cost optimisation, especially in off and minimal modes, is removing NAT gateways (where used) and replacing them with private endpoints to the cloud's managed services (key-value store, object storage, container registry). The platform requires no outbound internet egress for its read or write paths; running private-only is feasible.
A fresh deployment requires a one-time bootstrap to create the first administrator:

ol.li: Deploy the authoriser stack and the editing-pipeline stack.
Run a bootstrap script that:
Creates the first user in the IdP.
Maps that user's IdP group to a ceiling with the platform_admin role.
Creates a platform-admins group and adds the user to it.
The user can now sign in and manage everything else through the admin REST API.

p: After bootstrap, no further direct writes to the key-value store should be needed; the admin API covers all operations.
